Title: Why the Codex exists
Summary: The seven game domains are hardcoded. The codex is for everything else.
Body:
## The gap it fills

Bleepforge ships seven hardcoded game domains (NPCs, Items, Quests, Karma, Factions, Dialogs, Balloons). Each has a fixed schema, a tres mapper, and a dedicated edit page. That works great for the things you have already designed.

But mid project you discover concepts that do not fit any of those: Hazards, Locations, Vehicles, Mechanics, Acts. They have their own properties (a Hazard has a damage value, a duration, a sprite, a sound), and you want to author them somewhere structured rather than in scattered notes.

The Codex is that somewhere.

## What it is not

The Codex is not a parallel production pipeline. It does not round trip to Godot. Entries live entirely in JSON under `data/codex/<category>/`. If a Codex category becomes load bearing enough to deserve a real schema in the runtime, the right move is to graduate it: implement a Godot resource, write the tres mapper, build a dedicated edit page, then delete the Codex category.

The Codex's role is the staging ground for that graduation, not a permanent home.

## What it is good at

- Capturing project specific concepts before you decide on their final shape.
- Keeping references to assets and other entities (a Hazard category can reference Items and NPCs through ref properties).
- Living next to your real authoring surfaces, so the project bible is not in a separate Notion you forget to update.
